title: Kouign-amann
subtitle: Breton caramelised butter-and-sugar yeasted pastry
excerpt: A Breton specialty made from a simple yeasted dough into which cold butter and sugar are laminated through a series of folds, similar to croissant dough but simpler and faster. During baking the sugar caramelises between the layers and on the base, creating a shattering caramel crust and a chewy, buttery interior. The cake is best eaten warm on the day it is baked.
sourceNotes: Kouign-amann (Breton for butter cake) was created in 1860 by baker Yves-René Scordia in Douarnenez, Finistère, Brittany, reportedly from necessity when he had limited ingredients. The name and origin are documented in Breton food history records. The recipe entered wider French culinary literature through the twentieth century as a regional Breton speciality. The basic lamination method and the sugar-caramelisation bake are consistent across sources from that period.

content.text: The character of kouign-amann comes from

: cold butter and sugar are folded into the risen dough through three turns. Keep everything cold and work quickly. The most common failure is the butter melting into the dough; if this starts to happen, refrigerate immediately.

attrs.prepNote: very cold, cut into 1cm cubes, for lamination

content.text: Mix the flour, yeast, and salt in a large bowl. Add the warm water and mix to a rough dough. Knead for 5 to 6 minutes until smooth and slightly tacky. Place in a lightly oiled bowl, cover, and leave to rise in a warm place for 1 hour until doubled. While the dough rises, keep the butter cold in the fridge.

Butter and sugar the 23cm tin. Turn the risen dough onto a lightly floured surface. Roll out to a rough rectangle about 35 x 25 cm. Scatter half the cold butter cubes and half the sugar over the centre two-thirds. Fold the top third down over the filling, then fold the bottom third up (a letter fold). Press the edges. Rotate 90 degrees, roll out again, and repeat with the remaining butter and sugar. Fold again and refrigerate for 20 minutes.

Heat the oven to 200°C conventional (180°C fan). Roll the dough into a circle to fit the tin. Press into the buttered tin; the dough will resist at first but will relax after a minute. Sprinkle the flaky salt over the top. Leave to prove for 20 to 30 minutes until the dough looks slightly puffed. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until deep amber on top and caramelised at the base. Immediately invert onto a rack with a tray underneath to catch dripping caramel. Leave the tin in place for 2 minutes so the caramel drips back onto the cake. Remove the tin. Eat warm.
